title: The Potato Chip Was Invented in England in 1817 — 36 Years Before the American Story You've Heard
excerpt: The George Crum/Saratoga Springs story is the one everyone knows. It's also, historians say, a popularization story — not an invention story. Here's what actually predates it, and why "chip" means two different foods depending which side of the Atlantic you're on.
takeaways: "Chip" (US/Canada) and "crisp" (UK/Ireland) name the same thin-fried-potato snack — but in British English, "chip" instead means what Americans call French fries, making it a genuinely confusing cross-Atlantic food-vocabulary split.

The popular origin story credits chef George Crum with inventing the potato chip in 1853 at Saratoga Springs after a customer complaint — but historians are clear he was not the first to make one, only the one who popularized it.

The earliest documented recipe for something resembling a modern potato chip is English: William Kitchiner's 1817 cookbook The Cook's Oracle included "potatoes fried in slices or shavings" — 36 years before the Crum story.

An 1849 New York Herald article referenced a Saratoga cook named Eliza with a specific reputation for potato frying — four years before Crum's famous incident, suggesting the dish was already locally known before his version of the story.

content.text: Almost everyone who's heard the potato chip origin story knows the same version: a fussy railroad tycoon, an annoyed chef, a plate of potatoes sliced spitefully thin. It's a great story. It's also, according to food historians, not really the invention story it gets told as.
Two Words, One Snack — Sort Of

Start with the naming split itself, since it trips up more people than it should. Thin-sliced, fried, packaged potato snacks are called "chips" in North America and "crisps" in the UK and Ireland. But British English also uses "chips" — for a completely different food: what Americans call French fries. So the same word, depending which side of the Atlantic you're standing on, refers to two genuinely different potato products. Charles Dickens even used "husky chips of potato" in A Tale of Two Cities (1859) — referring to the thick-cut fried-potato-strip food, not the crunchy snack, even though the crunchy chip had already emerged as a distinct dish by that point.

The most widely told origin account centers on George Crum, born George Speck in 1824, a chef of African American and Native American (Huron) descent working at Moon's Lake House in Saratoga Springs, New York. According to the popular version, in 1853 railroad magnate Cornelius Vanderbilt sent back an order of fried potatoes, complaining the slices were too thick. Crum, in one telling annoyed enough to make a point of it, sliced potatoes paper-thin, fried them until crisp and impossible to eat with a fork, and sent them out — intending it as something close to a prank. Vanderbilt, instead of being offended, loved them. Other diners started requesting the same dish, and the restaurant began serving "Saratoga chips" as a standing menu item.

Historians are clear on one point: Crum did not invent the thin-fried-potato-slice concept, even though he's broadly credited with popularizing it. The earliest documented recipe resembling a modern potato chip is English, not American, and predates the Saratoga story by 36 years. In 1817, physician William Kitchiner published The Cook's Oracle, a cookbook that included a recipe for "potatoes fried in slices or shavings" — a description that lines up closely with what would later be called a potato chip, decades before Crum's supposed invention moment.

There's also a closer, more local complication: an 1849 New York Herald article referenced "Eliza," a Saratoga cook with a specific, apparently well-known reputation for potato frying — four years before Crum's famous 1853 incident. That reference suggests thin-fried-potato preparation already had some local recognition in the Saratoga Springs area before the version of the story most people know takes place.

The scholarly position threads the needle carefully, and it's a more honest version of the story than the pure-invention myth: Crum almost certainly did not invent the potato chip, but he does appear to have been the one who turned an existing, relatively obscure preparation into a named, commercially recognizable dish — the Saratoga chip — that then spread more widely through American food culture from that specific restaurant and moment. Popularizer, not inventor. It's a less tidy story than the customer-complaint anecdote, but it's the one that actually survives scrutiny of the documented record.

content.text: Sources: Smithsonian Magazine, "The Curious History of the Potato Chip"; PBS Food, "Baked Potato Chips and George Crum"; Lemelson-MIT Program, George Crum profile; William Kitchiner, The Cook's Oracle (1817).
